h1: Lease vs. Buy: Compare Car Financing Options
description: Compare leasing vs auto loans side by side. Calculate monthly payments and find the best car financing option for you.
intro: Deciding between leasing and financing a car is a major financial decision. Use this calculator to compare monthly payments, total costs, and ownership benefits. The calculations account for 2026 pricing and are updated regularly to help you make an informed choice.
keyTakeaway: Compare total costs: lease payments vs. loan payments with interest. Understanding both options helps you choose the right financing strategy for your budget and driving needs.

fields.label: Loan Term (Years)
fields.help: Financing period in years. Longer terms result in lower monthly payments but higher total interest.

explanation:
Car financing decisions require understanding the costs and benefits of each option. Whether you lease or finance through an auto loan depends on your mileage, budget, and desired ownership experience.

## How it works

This calculator compares the monthly payments and total costs of leasing versus buying a car with an auto loan. It helps you understand the financial implications of each option based on your vehicle price and loan term.

## Final notes

This calculation is for reference only. For major financial decisions, consult with a financial advisor or loan officer. Rates and terms are current as of 2026 and subject to change.

faq.q: What's the main difference between leasing and financing a car?
faq.a: Leasing is renting; financing is buying. With a lease, you make monthly payments but never own the car. With financing, you pay off a loan and own the vehicle when it's paid off.

faq.q: Is leasing or buying better for me?
faq.a: It depends. Lease if you like new cars, drive under 12k-15k miles yearly, and prefer predictable costs. Buy if you drive more, want to customize your car, or keep it long-term.

faq.q: What's included in my monthly lease payment?
faq.a: Typically: vehicle depreciation, interest (called the money factor), and taxes. Maintenance and wear-and-tear coverage are often included, but insurance and registration may not be.

faq.q: What are typical lease mileage limits?
faq.a: Most leases allow 10,000-15,000 miles per year. Exceeding this usually costs $0.25-$0.35 per extra mile, which can add up significantly.

faq.q: Can I buy a leased car at the end?
faq.a: Most leases include a purchase option (residual value set at signing). You can buy it, but the price is often higher than the current market value.

faq.q: Is leasing tax deductible if I own a business?
faq.a: Yes. Business lease payments are 100% tax deductible. For financing, only the interest portion is deductible, not the principal payment.

faq.q: What happens if I exceed my mileage allowance?
faq.a: You'll pay an overage charge (typically $0.20-$0.35 per mile) when you return the vehicle. This can get expensive with high mileage.

faq.q: What's better: leasing or financing for a long-term car?
faq.a: Financing is better if you keep cars 5+ years or drive over 12,000 miles yearly. Leasing is better if you like a new car every 2-4 years.

faq.q: How do I calculate the true cost of ownership?
faq.a: Add: monthly payments + insurance + maintenance + fuel + taxes. Leasing typically has lower payments but comes with mileage restrictions.

sources.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
